Epistemology: A friend says, "I know it is true because everyone is saying it." What would you check first?
Two teachers give different answers about the same event. How would you decide which answer is stronger?
You read a confident post online with no source attached. What would make it worth trusting?
A family story has been repeated for years. What evidence would help you know whether it is accurate?
Someone changes their mind after seeing new facts. When is that wisdom instead of weakness?
A video looks real but could be edited. What clues would help you decide?
A doctor, a friend, and an influencer all give advice. How would you rank their credibility?
You believe something strongly because it feels obvious. What test could challenge that belief?
A rumor turns out to be partly true. What would you still need to know before accepting the full story?
What is one belief you would be willing to update if better evidence showed up?
A podcast guest says they did their own research. What would you need to see before trusting the conclusion?
A photo seems to prove a claim, but you do not know when it was taken. What should you verify?
Someone says, "I saw it with my own eyes." When can eyewitness evidence still be wrong?
A poll says most people agree. What does that tell you, and what does it not tell you?
A person refuses to believe a fact because they dislike the source. How should the claim be checked separately from the source?
Your first answer feels right immediately. What would be a fair way to slow down and test it?
A news article links to a study. What would you check in the study before trusting the article?
Someone says a claim is impossible because it has never happened before. Is that enough evidence?
A friend says, "Trust me, I know someone who was there." What follow-up question would help?
An expert changes their recommendation after new data. When should that increase your trust?
A belief makes you feel safe. How could that feeling make the belief harder to question?
A group repeats the same idea until it feels true. How would you separate repetition from evidence?
A claim is partly outside your knowledge. How would you decide who to ask?
Someone uses a personal story to prove a general rule. What else would you need?
A website has a professional design but no author listed. How much should design affect trust?
You find two sources that disagree. What would make one source more reliable than the other?
A prediction came true once. What would show whether the person is actually good at predicting?
A belief has helped you make decisions for years. What would still justify questioning it?
Someone says, "There is no proof it is false." Why is that not the same as proof it is true?
What is one current opinion you should label as uncertain instead of settled?
Philosophy of History: After Rome falls, one person blames weak leaders and another blames economic pressure. Which explanation is stronger, and why?
A history textbook calls someone a hero, while another calls the same person dangerous. What facts would you compare?
During the American Revolution, how would a loyalist and a patriot tell the same event differently?
When studying an empire, what matters more: the ruler's personality, the economy, or the institutions?
A country wins a war and writes the official story. What evidence might be missing?
How would you judge a historical figure who did something normal then but harmful by today's standards?
What lesson from the fall of a powerful civilization could apply to a modern company or country?
If a leader gets credit for a movement, how would you check whether conditions made the movement possible?
How can maps, monuments, and holidays shape what people remember about history?
Pick a historical event you know. What is one question that would make the explanation less one-sided?
When Napoleon rose to power, how much came from his skill and how much from the chaos around him?
If you studied the Civil Rights Movement, what would be lost by focusing only on famous leaders?
A museum exhibit leaves out the losing side of a conflict. What question would you ask the curator?
How could the same industrial revolution look like progress to factory owners and suffering to workers?
When a country builds a monument, what story is it trying to preserve?
A king is remembered as great because the kingdom expanded. What costs might that memory hide?
How would a merchant, a soldier, and a farmer describe the same war differently?
When studying colonization, what evidence should come from the people who were colonized?
A revolution promises freedom but creates violence. How should both truths be held together?
What does the Black Death show about how disaster can change economics and power?
If a historical source was written by a government official, what bias might it contain?
How could geography shape a civilization's choices before any leader made a decision?
Why might ordinary people's diaries teach something a king's speech cannot?
When a society collapses, what signs would you look for before blaming one person?
How does technology, like the printing press, change who gets to influence history?
What modern event might be explained differently 100 years from now?
How should we judge a reformer who improved one thing but ignored another injustice?
What would make a historical comparison useful instead of lazy?
How can a nation remember a victory without becoming blind to its mistakes?
What historical lesson do people quote too easily without checking the details?
Logic & Debate: Someone says, "My team won after I wore this shirt, so the shirt is lucky." What is wrong with the reasoning?
In a classroom debate, one person attacks the speaker instead of the argument. How would you bring it back to the claim?
A friend says, "If you disagree with me, you must support the opposite." What third option might exist?
Someone gives one dramatic example and treats it like proof. What question should you ask?
You are arguing for a school policy change. What claim, evidence, and counterargument would you prepare?
A debate opponent makes one good point. How can you admit that without giving up your whole position?
Someone uses a big statistic without explaining where it came from. What would you ask next?
A person says, "This happened before, so it will happen again." What would make that comparison fair or unfair?
How would you explain the strongest argument against your own view in a way your opponent would respect?
What sentence in your argument needs the clearest definition before anyone can judge it?
Someone says, "All politicians lie, so this politician is lying." What is missing from that argument?
A student says homework should be banned because one assignment was pointless. What is the weak leap?
A friend says, "You are either with us or against us." How would you challenge that framing?
In a debate about phones in school, what evidence would matter more than personal annoyance?
Someone uses a rare scary story to argue for a big rule. What base-rate question should you ask?
A person changes the subject when their claim is challenged. How would you return to the original point?
Someone says, "It worked for me, so it will work for everyone." What should be tested?
A speaker uses a confident tone but gives no evidence. How should confidence be weighed?
A debate gets heated and both sides exaggerate. What claim could each side make more precise?
Someone says, "Experts have been wrong before." Why does that not automatically defeat expert evidence?
A person argues that because an idea is old, it must be wise. What counterexample could test that?
A person argues that because an idea is new, it must be better. What counterexample could test that?
If your argument depends on one statistic, what should you know about how it was measured?
How would you steelman a view you strongly dislike before criticizing it?
Someone gives three weak reasons instead of one strong reason. How would you identify the best one?
What part of an argument should be defined before people start arguing about examples?
A person says, "That is just common sense." What question would turn common sense into an argument?
How can you disagree with a conclusion while admitting one premise is true?
A friend asks for your honest opinion but only wants agreement. How would you separate kindness from honesty?
What would make you say, "I still disagree, but that was a strong argument"?
Strategic Thinking: In chess, a player sacrifices a piece to win later. What is a real-life version of that kind of trade?
A student can win an argument today or protect a friendship long term. What should they consider?
A business lowers prices to beat a competitor. What second problem might that create later?
A coach benches a star player for breaking a rule. Why might that help the whole team later?
You can act quickly with little information or wait for better information. What would decide the choice?
A leader stays quiet during a conflict instead of responding immediately. When could that be strategic?
What is one move that looks slow now but could create a stronger position later?
If you had only one chance to ask a question before making a decision, what would you ask?
What choice in your life is reversible, and what choice would be hard to undo?
What would a patient opponent notice about your plan that you might miss?
A basketball team slows the game down against a faster team. What advantage are they trying to create?
A company refuses a quick profit to protect its brand. When is that wise?
A student chooses an easier class to protect their GPA. What long-term cost might appear?
A negotiator lets the other side speak first. What information might they gain?
A general avoids battle even though the army wants action. What could make restraint the better move?
A founder turns down investors to keep control. What tradeoff are they making?
A person apologizes early to stop a conflict from growing. When is that strategic rather than weak?
A team practices basics instead of flashy plays. Why might that win later?
A player reveals less of their plan than they know. When is information worth protecting?
You can choose the popular path or the path with fewer competitors. What should you compare?
A business expands quickly before training managers. What second problem might appear?
A student studies the teacher's test style before studying everything equally. Is that smart or shallow?
A person keeps options open too long and misses the moment. When does flexibility become avoidance?
A leader gives a rival a small win to prevent a larger fight. When could that work?
What would make a temporary loss worth accepting?
What resource in your life is scarce: time, money, attention, trust, or energy?
If everyone can see your next move, how might that weaken it?
What plan depends too much on other people acting reasonably?
Where would a backup plan change your confidence?
What decision should be delayed until you know one more fact?
Ethics & Values: In Les Miserables, Jean Valjean steals bread to survive. How should need, law, and harm be weighed?
A friend tells you a secret that could hurt someone else. When should loyalty give way to responsibility?
A school rule is fair for most students but unfair for one unusual case. Should the rule bend?
A company can make more money by hiding a small risk. What value should guide the decision?
Someone apologizes only after getting caught. How should intention and outcome be judged?
A leader must choose between honesty and keeping people calm. What should come first?
A teammate cheats to help your group win. What do you owe the team, the rules, and yourself?
A city can help many people but will harm a small neighborhood. What questions should be asked before deciding?
When is forgiveness wise, and when does it allow the same harm to continue?
What value would you still defend if it cost you popularity?
In Antigone, a character must choose between law and family duty. Which duty should come first?
A school can punish a whole class because no one admits who broke a rule. Is that fair?
A friend asks you to lie to protect them from consequences. What value is being tested?
A company donates money publicly after causing harm privately. How should that be judged?
A doctor has limited medicine for two patients. What should decide who receives it?
A person tells a harsh truth mainly to hurt someone. Does truthfulness excuse cruelty?
A team wins because the referee missed a call. Should the players say something?
A rule treats everyone the same but affects one group worse. Is equal treatment always fair?
A person steals expensive medicine they cannot afford. How should law and survival be weighed?
A leader hides bad news to prevent panic. When does protection become manipulation?
Someone forgives a repeated harm without setting a boundary. What value is missing?
A student reports cheating and loses friends. What makes the choice worth it or not?
A city removes a historic statue. What values are in conflict?
A person gives charity for attention. Does motive change the value of the act?
A promise becomes harmful to keep. When should a promise be broken?
An employee exposes company wrongdoing and risks their job. What duties are competing?
A parent favors honesty but punishes every confession harshly. What behavior will that create?
A popular person is treated more gently than everyone else. What should justice require?
What value would you not trade for comfort?
What ethical rule sounds good until you imagine being on the weaker side?
Literature & Wisdom: In The Odyssey, Odysseus is clever but often proud. When does cleverness become a weakness?
In Macbeth, ambition turns destructive. What warning sign shows ambition is becoming dangerous?
In Animal Farm, language is used to control people. What modern example feels similar?
In Romeo and Juliet, emotion moves faster than judgment. What decision needed more time?
In To Kill a Mockingbird, courage is not just physical. What does moral courage look like today?
In The Great Gatsby, a dream becomes an illusion. How can a person tell hope from self-deception?
In a story where a character betrays a friend, what motive would you look for besides simple evil?
What character from a book or movie learns too late? What should they have noticed earlier?
What story shows a person confusing pride with strength?
Which fictional mistake have you seen people repeat in real life?
In Frankenstein, Victor creates something powerful and avoids responsibility. Where do people do that today?
In Lord of the Flies, rules collapse when fear grows. What keeps groups civilized?
In The Crucible, fear and reputation drive accusations. What modern situation has that pattern?
In Hamlet, overthinking delays action. When does reflection become paralysis?
In Pride and Prejudice, first impressions mislead people. What first impression have you seen fail?
In The Hunger Games, survival changes what people think is acceptable. How does pressure shape morality?
In The Lion King, Simba avoids responsibility after shame. What does avoidance cost him?
In The Lord of the Rings, power tempts even good people. What makes power dangerous?
In a tragedy, the hero often has one flaw. What flaw causes the most damage in real life?
In a comedy, characters often misunderstand each other. What does that reveal about communication?
In myths, heroes often ignore warnings. Why do warnings become easier to dismiss when people want glory?
In a coming-of-age story, what usually has to die: innocence, pride, or fear?
What story shows that revenge can control the person seeking it?
What character mistakes attention for love or respect?
What story shows someone learning humility too late?
What villain believes they are doing the right thing? How should that be judged?
What fictional mentor gives advice that sounds simple but is hard to live?
What story shows the danger of wanting to belong too badly?
What character is wise because they know what not to chase?
Which story would help someone understand your current problem?
Worldview & Cultures: In one culture, direct honesty is respected; in another, indirect speech protects harmony. How should people avoid misunderstanding each other?
A tradition seems strange from the outside. What should you learn before judging it?
Two families define success differently: one values independence, another values duty. How can both make sense?
A person moves to a new country and misreads social rules. What questions should they ask first?
A holiday means pride to one group and pain to another. How should both views be understood?
When does respecting another worldview become avoiding honest disagreement?
What belief from your background feels obvious but might not feel obvious elsewhere?
A culture values elders' advice more than individual choice. What are the strengths and risks?
How can food, language, and family roles shape what people think is normal?
What question would help you understand a worldview you disagree with?
A guest refuses food because of religion, and the host feels rejected. How could both sides understand each other?
In some cultures, children are expected to care for parents at home. What values support that expectation?
A workplace has people from direct and indirect communication cultures. What conflict might happen?
A person from an individualist culture joins a collectivist family. What might they misread?
A culture treats time strictly; another treats time flexibly. What values might each protect?
A student is quiet in class because respect means listening first. How could a teacher misinterpret that?
A tradition limits individual choice but strengthens community. How should both sides be weighed?
A person judges another culture's clothing rules too quickly. What should they learn first?
A society values honor, another values personal freedom. Where might they clash?
A family expects career choices to serve the family, not just the individual. What worldview is underneath?
How can translation change the meaning of a belief or argument?
A holiday is joyful for one group and painful for another. How should public memory handle that?
When does cultural respect become an excuse to ignore harm?
What custom from your background would confuse an outsider?
How could food rules reveal deeper beliefs about purity, identity, or belonging?
What can a funeral tradition reveal about a culture's view of life?
When does assimilation ask too much of a person?
How can two cultures disagree about success and both be reasonable?
What question would you ask before judging a tradition harshly?
Where might your own culture make you blind?
Media Literacy: A headline says, "Everyone is furious." What would you check before believing that?
A viral clip starts in the middle of an argument. What missing context could change the story?
A celebrity gives advice about health, money, or politics. What makes them credible or not credible?
A creator posts an emotional story and sells a product right after. What incentive should you notice?
A chart looks convincing but has no source. What would you ask before sharing it?
A news story uses one shocking photo. How could the image shape your reaction before you read?
A trend makes an opinion popular overnight. What social reward might be driving it?
Two outlets report the same event with different words. Which words reveal the framing?
A meme makes a serious issue funny. What does it clarify, and what does it hide?
What is one question you should ask before reposting something that makes you angry?
A video caption tells you what to feel before the clip starts. How does that shape your reaction?
An influencer says, "They do not want you to know this." What suspicion is being triggered?
A news story quotes one angry person as if they represent everyone. What should you check?
A thumbnail uses a shocked face and a red arrow. What is it trying to do to your attention?
A post uses the word "destroyed" for a normal disagreement. What framing is happening?
A creator reviews a product they were paid to promote. What disclosure matters?
A clip removes the question before the answer. How could that change the meaning?
A rumor spreads because it confirms what people already dislike. Why is that dangerous?
A statistic sounds huge but lacks a denominator. What number is missing?
A political ad shows scary music and dark images. What emotion is being used?
A viral challenge looks harmless but encourages risky behavior. What incentive is the platform creating?
A headline asks a question instead of making a claim. Why might that be used?
A creator builds trust with jokes, then gives serious advice. What should still be checked?
A story is technically true but leaves out timing. How can timing change the meaning?
A celebrity apology video appears after a sponsorship loss. What incentive should you notice?
A post says "mainstream media is silent" but many outlets covered it. What should you verify?
A graph uses colors that make one side look dangerous. How can design persuade?
A trend rewards people for being extreme. What kind of claims will spread fastest?
A meme makes one side look stupid. What argument is being avoided?
What is your personal rule for sharing emotional content?
People & Leadership: A team member takes credit for group work. How should the leader respond without creating drama?
A friend is disrespectful in public but apologizes in private. What boundary would be fair?
A coach lets the best player break rules. What message does the team receive?
A manager avoids hard feedback to stay liked. What problem grows over time?
Two friends are arguing, and both want you to take their side. What would a fair response look like?
A leader has bad news to share. What should be said clearly, and what should be handled privately?
Someone acts confident but keeps avoiding responsibility. What signal should you watch?
A group rewards the loudest person, not the best idea. How can a leader change that?
When does kindness need a boundary to stay useful?
How do you tell the difference between loyalty and covering for someone?
A captain praises only the top scorer while the defense did the hard work. What behavior will that create?
A leader says, "My door is always open," but punishes bad news. What will people hide?
A friend keeps joking after being asked to stop. What boundary should be repeated?
A manager gives vague criticism like "be better." What feedback would actually help?
A group chat turns against one person. What would a responsible leader do?
A team member is quiet but has the best idea. How can the group make space for it?
A leader takes credit when things go well and blames others when they fail. What trust problem appears?
A friend apologizes but changes nothing. What should you look for next?
A teacher enforces rules for some students but not others. What happens to respect?
A coach must choose between a talented selfish player and a less talented team player. What matters?
A leader shares too much private information to seem transparent. Where is the line?
A person uses loyalty to avoid accountability. How can you name that without attacking them?
A group rewards confidence more than preparation. What mistakes will follow?
A leader delays a hard decision until everyone is frustrated. What did delay cost?
A teammate needs correction in public. When should correction happen privately?
A friend is hurt but will not say what they need. What question could open the conversation?
A team succeeds once and becomes arrogant. What should a leader remind them?
A person dominates every meeting. How can a leader protect better thinking?
A leader wants to be liked by everyone. What decision will become hardest?
What behavior should a leader reward if they want honesty?
Creative Thinking: A musician has only three chords. How could the limit make the song more creative?
A designer cannot use color. What other tools could make the design work?
A writer is stuck on a story ending. What would happen if the hero failed instead of won?
A business has no money for ads. What creative way could it earn attention?
A student has to explain science without slides. What object or story could make the idea clear?
A chef is missing the main ingredient. How could the constraint create a better dish?
What boring rule in a project could be turned into a strength?
How would a beginner solve a problem differently from an expert?
When should you stop brainstorming and test one idea?
What idea becomes stronger when you make it simpler?
A filmmaker has no budget for special effects. How could suspense replace spectacle?
A teacher has to explain fractions with pizza, music, or sports. Which example would you use?
A game designer removes one button from a game. How could that make gameplay better?
A poet cannot use the word love. What images could show it instead?
A store has a tiny space. How could layout become part of the brand?
A student must present history as a courtroom trial. What roles would make it memorable?
A band loses its singer before a show. What creative pivot could still work?
A product is useful but boring. What story could make people care?
A city wall is covered in graffiti. How could the problem become a community art project?
A writer's villain feels flat. What desire or fear would make the villain more human?
A chef has leftovers and one spice. What makes the constraint interesting?
A science fair project failed. How could the failure become the main discovery?
A logo must work in black and white. What design choices matter most?
A classroom has no technology for a lesson. How could movement or debate replace slides?
A story is too predictable. What expectation could be reversed?
A person copies a popular style. What twist would make it their own?
A problem has been solved the same way for years. What assumption should be broken?
A beginner asks a naive question. How could that question reveal a better approach?
What idea would improve if you removed half of it?
What ordinary object could explain a difficult idea?
Work & Ambition: A worker stays late every night but avoids the hardest task. Is that ambition or avoidance?
A student wants credit for a group project. Who needs to see the work, and when?
A person takes a promotion that pays more but teaches less. What should they consider?
A mentor gives advice that worked 20 years ago. How would you decide whether it still fits?
A teammate is busy all day but produces little. What system would improve the work?
A person wants success quickly and skips skill-building. What cost might appear later?
When does asking for recognition help your reputation, and when does it hurt it?
What skill would raise your ceiling more than working extra hours?
A shortcut gets fast results but creates weak habits. When is it worth it?
What should someone stop doing if they want real progress faster?
A student joins too many clubs to look impressive. What signal might colleges or employers actually notice?
An employee answers emails instantly but avoids deep work. What is the hidden tradeoff?
A person wants to start a business but only watches advice videos. What action would prove seriousness?
A worker complains about no promotion but never asks what skills are missing. What should they do first?
A student picks a major only because it sounds prestigious. What questions should they ask?
A person works hard for a boss who never gives credit. What leverage can they build?
A teammate does invisible work that keeps the project alive. How should they make it visible?
A person says yes to every opportunity and becomes average at all of them. What should they cut?
A job pays well but drains energy and growth. What would make it worth staying?
A person wants to be known as reliable. What repeated behavior proves it?
A student waits until motivation appears. What system would work without motivation?
A worker gets feedback that feels unfair. How can they find the useful part anyway?
A person chases a title instead of skills. What risk appears later?
A side project could teach more than a class. How would you judge the opportunity?
A person avoids networking because it feels fake. What would make it genuine?
A team rewards the person who talks most. How can quiet competence be shown?
A person is busy all day but cannot name the main result. What should change?
A mentor's path worked in a different economy. How should advice be adjusted?
A person wants faster progress. What habit should they stop protecting?
What work would still matter if nobody praised it this week?
Financial Judgment: A teenager wants to buy a phone with monthly payments. What cost matters besides the sticker price?
A friend says, "This investment cannot lose." What question should you ask first?
Someone gets a raise and immediately raises their spending. What risk appears later?
A business can borrow money to grow faster. What would make the debt smart or dangerous?
You can save money now or buy something that may help you earn later. How would you compare the choices?
A sale says 40% off, but you did not need the item. What is the real cost?
A risky choice has a big upside. What downside would you need to survive?
A person assumes they will earn more next year. How could that assumption distort today's decision?
When does waiting create financial value instead of just delaying?
What evidence would make you trust a financial opportunity more?
A person buys a car because the monthly payment looks affordable. What total costs should they calculate?
A friend wants to invest because everyone online is excited. What would you ask before joining?
A subscription is only ten dollars a month. When does small become expensive?
A person uses a credit card for points but carries a balance. What is the real tradeoff?
A business spends money on branding before proving people want the product. What risk is that?
A teenager gets birthday money. How could they split spending, saving, and learning?
A stock drops and someone wants to sell from fear. What information matters before deciding?
A person gets a bonus and wants to upgrade everything. What future need should be considered first?
A cheap item breaks three times. When is expensive actually cheaper?
A loan helps someone go to school. What would make the debt reasonable?
A person ignores insurance because nothing bad has happened. What reasoning mistake is that?
A business owner can hire help or keep all profit. What opportunity cost exists?
A discount ends tonight. How does urgency affect judgment?
A friend asks to borrow money again. What boundary protects both people?
A person compares salary but ignores commute, stress, and growth. What else should count?
A risky opportunity could double money or lose half. What downside can they survive?
A person assumes rent will stay the same. How could that distort a budget?
A family wants a vacation and has credit card debt. What priorities should be named?
A financial influencer shows luxury results but not failures. What evidence is missing?
What purchase would you delay for 48 hours before deciding?
Science & Evidence: A study says coffee makes people live longer. What else might explain the result?
A medicine works for one person you know. Why is that not enough proof for everyone?
A graph looks dramatic because the scale starts near the top. How does that change your reaction?
A class experiment works once. What would repeating it prove?
A company funds research about its own product. What should you check?
A headline says two things are linked. What would show one actually causes the other?
A sample includes only volunteers. How could that bias the result?
A weather forecast says 70% chance of rain. What does that mean in practical terms?
What would a better experiment control for?
What result would make you update your view?
A fitness influencer says one supplement caused their results. What variables are missing?
A class plant grows better near the window. What would you control before claiming sunlight caused it?
A survey asks only people in one neighborhood. What conclusion would be too broad?
A company says 9 out of 10 users improved. What should you know about the tenth and the sample?
A study on sleep uses self-reported hours. What measurement problem could appear?
A social media post says cold weather causes colds. What would a better explanation include?
A graph shows two lines rising together. What would prove one caused the other?
A new medicine helps in early tests. Why do larger trials matter?
A school changes lunch and test scores rise. What else could explain the change?
A scientist fails to repeat another lab's result. Why is that important?
A headline says chocolate improves memory. What would you check before believing it?
A sports team wins after a new training routine. What comparison group would help?
A weather app says 30% chance of rain. How should you use that information?
A study excludes people who dropped out. How could that change the result?
A product is labeled natural. Why is that not the same as proven safe?
A chart uses averages. What individual differences might be hidden?
A researcher expects one result. How can blind testing help?
A result is statistically significant. What else should you ask before caring?
A claim sounds scientific because it uses complex words. What evidence still matters?
What everyday claim could you test with a simple experiment?
Technology & AI: A student uses AI to write an essay. What learning might be lost, and what help might be useful?
A company uses an algorithm to choose who gets interviews. What could go wrong?
A smart device makes life easier but collects private data. What tradeoff should be considered?
A navigation app always picks the fastest route. What might it ignore?
A chatbot gives a confident answer that is wrong. Who should be responsible for checking it?
A school bans phones completely. What problem does that solve, and what problem might it create?
A tool saves time so people stop practicing a skill. When does convenience become dependency?
An app rewards attention, not well-being. What behavior will it train?
What should AI refuse to optimize, even if users ask for it?
What human judgment should stay involved when technology makes a recommendation?
A teacher lets students use AI for brainstorming but not final answers. What line is being drawn?
A hiring algorithm rejects applicants with employment gaps. Who might be unfairly hurt?
A facial recognition system works worse for some groups. What should happen before using it?
A family uses a smart speaker in every room. What convenience and privacy tradeoff exists?
A student trusts a calculator answer without understanding the math. What skill is at risk?
A company replaces customer service workers with bots. What improves, and what gets worse?
A social app recommends more extreme videos to keep attention. What incentive drives that?
A school uses software to flag cheating. What mistakes could harm innocent students?
An AI image looks real. What responsibility does the person sharing it have?
A map app sends traffic through quiet neighborhoods. Who pays the cost of convenience?
A person uses AI as a therapist. What help might it offer, and what danger remains?
A company collects data to personalize ads. When does personalization become manipulation?
A child learns from a tablet more than from adults. What should parents watch for?
A tool makes writing faster but more generic. What human choice should remain?
A robot can care for elderly people physically. What emotional needs might be missed?
A platform removes harmful posts. What free-speech questions appear?
A deepfake damages someone's reputation. What proof should people demand before reacting?
A person automates a task they do not understand. What failure will be hard to catch?
A device updates automatically and changes features. Who controls the product after purchase?
What decision should never be fully handed to an algorithm?
Health & Habits: A person wants to sleep better but keeps their phone beside the bed. What cue is hurting the habit?
Someone starts a hard workout plan and quits in a week. What smaller system might last?
A student snacks when stressed, not hungry. What reward is the habit giving?
A person says, "I just need more discipline." What environment change might help more?
A habit works during calm weeks but fails during busy weeks. What should the plan account for?
Someone wants to eat healthier but shops while hungry. What decision should happen earlier?
What small friction would make a bad habit harder?
What identity story helps a healthy habit stick?
When are you most likely to break a plan, and why?
What evidence would show whether your habit plan is realistic?
A student says they will study more but keeps notifications on. What environment change comes first?
A person starts running five days a week after doing none. What smaller version might last?
Someone eats late because they skip breakfast. What earlier habit might solve the later problem?
A person sleeps poorly but drinks caffeine all afternoon. What pattern should they test?
A habit tracker becomes a source of shame. How could it be redesigned?
A person wants less screen time but uses the phone as an alarm. What setup change could help?
A friend says stress makes them snap at people. What recovery habit might protect relationships?
A person keeps junk food visible and fruit hidden. What choice architecture is working against them?
Someone quits after missing one workout. What belief about failure needs changing?
A person wants to meditate but hates sitting still. What alternative could train the same skill?
A team wants healthier lunches but only offers pizza at meetings. What incentive is being created?
A person feels tired and assumes laziness. What physical factors should they check?
A habit works on weekdays but fails on weekends. What plan is missing?
A student studies in bed and keeps falling asleep. What context cue is confusing the brain?
A person rewards every hard day with the habit they want to quit. What replacement reward could work?
A goal says "get healthy." What measurable behavior would make it real?
A person wants discipline but has no bedtime. What foundation comes first?
A friend copies a celebrity diet. What personal differences matter?
A person is embarrassed to start small. Why might small be smarter?
What habit would become easier if you changed the room first?
Civic Thinking: A city wants more safety cameras. What privacy tradeoff should citizens debate?
A school changes a rule to help most students, but a few are hurt by it. What should leaders consider?
A tax pays for a public service. Who benefits, who pays, and who might be missed?
A new law sounds good, but people may adapt around it. What incentive could it create?
A town argues over building housing. What tradeoff exists between current residents and future residents?
A protest blocks traffic to get attention. How should rights, disruption, and public impact be weighed?
When should local choice matter more than one national rule?
A policy works in one country. What would you check before copying it elsewhere?
What evidence would show whether a public policy actually works?
How would you judge the same rule if your preferred group lost power?
A city wants to ban cars downtown. Who benefits, who struggles, and what evidence should decide?
A school board debates later start times. What tradeoffs matter besides student preference?
A town raises taxes for better parks. How should citizens judge whether it is worth it?
A law is meant to stop crime but also increases police power. What safeguards matter?
A neighborhood opposes new housing. What interests of current and future residents conflict?
A country requires national service. What values support and challenge that idea?
A city removes library funding to lower taxes. What hidden costs might appear later?
A protest blocks a highway. How should attention, rights, and public harm be weighed?
A policy helps many people a little but hurts a few people a lot. How should that be judged?
A leader promises simple answers to a complex public problem. What should citizens ask?
A rule works well in a small town. What might fail when applied nationally?
A public health rule limits personal freedom. What evidence would justify it?
A city uses fines to fund services. Who is most likely to be burdened?
A candidate gives people what they want now but creates debt later. How should voters think about that?
A law has a good intention but poor enforcement. Should it be changed or removed?
A public school teaches a controversial book. Who should decide and by what standard?
A community wants safety and privacy. Where should the line be drawn?
A policy succeeds on average but fails for rural areas. What adjustment might be needed?
A government program is abused by some people but helps many others. How should abuse be weighed?
What local issue would you understand better by asking who pays and who benefits?
Personal Identity: A person says, "That is just who I am," after hurting someone. When is identity becoming an excuse?
Someone refuses to change an opinion because their friends know them for it. What are they protecting?
A student thinks being smart means never asking for help. What belief needs updating?
A person builds their whole identity around winning. What happens when they lose?
Someone changes their style, beliefs, or goals. When is that growth instead of being fake?
A person calls themselves loyal but avoids telling the truth. What value is being confused?
What label do you protect even when it stops helping you?
Which part of your self-image needs more evidence?
What would remain true about you if one opinion changed?
Where are you confusing consistency with loyalty to an old version of yourself?
A student known as "the smart one" avoids hard classes to protect that image. What identity trap is happening?
A person says, "I am just brutally honest," after being cruel. What label is protecting them?
Someone changes beliefs after meeting new people and gets called fake. When is change growth?
A person refuses help because they see themselves as independent. What cost can that identity create?
An athlete gets injured and feels worthless. What identity needs to expand?
A person stays in a group because leaving would change how others see them. What are they protecting?
Someone calls themselves loyal but ignores wrongdoing. What value is being confused?
A student thinks asking questions makes them look weak. What belief should be tested?
A person builds a personality around being funny and avoids serious conversations. What might be hidden?
A person wants to be seen as chill, so they never state boundaries. What cost appears?
A person keeps an old opinion because changing would feel embarrassing. What would humility look like?
A family expects someone to follow one path, but they want another. Which identity is theirs and which is inherited?
A person calls themselves a failure after one mistake. What evidence are they ignoring?
A student wants to be different from everyone else. When can that become its own kind of conformity?
A person uses past pain to explain every current choice. When does explanation become a cage?
Someone is proud of never quitting. When might quitting be wise?
A person wants confidence but avoids situations that would build it. What contradiction is present?
What label would you still be okay losing if it made you more honest?
What part of your self-image needs better evidence?
What would remain true about you if one important opinion changed?
